food_name: Chicken Strips in Breadcrumbs (Bastoncini di Pollo)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302757 covers breaded chicken as a category; no exact entry for Italian-style bastoncini di pollo strips exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers coated or breaded poultry meat; no exact code for bastoncini di pollo. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for breaded chicken strips as a prepared food product.

serving.description: 1 plate (chicken strips with potato wedges and tomato sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of bastoncini di pollo consists of approximately 350 g, comprising breaded chicken strips, hot potato wedges, and a side of tomato or spicy tomato sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, fried (USDA SR Legacy NDB 05063) and Bread crumbs, dry, grated, plain (USDA SR Legacy NDB 18079); composite estimate for breaded fried chicken strips
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central — SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry for bastoncini di pollo exists. Values estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for fried breaded chicken breast strips: approximately 60% cooked chicken breast meat and 40% breadcrumb coating by weight, adjusted for frying oil absorption. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are consistent with published values for breaded fried chicken strips (~220 kcal/100 g, ~17 g protein, ~10 g fat, ~15 g carbohydrate per 100 g). Potato wedges and sauce are served alongside but nutrient values here represent the chicken strip component only as the primary food item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; breaded/crumbed chicken products and white bread coating analogy
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bastoncini di pollo. GI estimated at 55 based on the analogous food category of breaded/crumbed chicken products, where the glycemic response is primarily driven by the breadcrumb coating (white bread GI ~70) moderated by the protein and fat content of the chicken, which significantly blunts the glycemic response. A composite GI of approximately 55 is consistent with published values for similar breaded meat products in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). GL per serving calculated as (55 × 15.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 350 g serving) / 100 = 28.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg/100 g) from breadcrumb coating, seasoning, and tomato sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce sodium intake, as a full 350 g serving provides approximately 1470 mg sodium, approaching the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; requesting sauce on the side and avoiding added salt can help moderate intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~55) from breadcrumb coating and moderate carbohydrate load per serving (~52.5 g per 350 g plate)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the total carbohydrate load from the breadcrumb coating and potato wedges; consuming a smaller portion of chicken strips and limiting potato wedges can help manage postprandial blood glucose, and pairing with a non-starchy vegetable side is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.3 g/100 g) and sodium (~420 mg/100 g) from frying and seasoned breadcrumb coating
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the saturated fat and sodium content of breaded fried chicken strips warrants portion control; opting for an oven-baked preparation and requesting low-sodium tomato sauce can reduce cardiovascular risk factors associated with this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bastoncini di Pollo, meaning 'chicken sticks' in Italian, are homemade-style Italian chicken strips made from tender chicken breast fillets coated in seasoned breadcrumbs and either shallow-fried or oven-baked until golden and crispy. The dish is a classic Italian comfort food preparation, popular in family-style Italian restaurants. At Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore in Singapore, the dish is served with hot potato wedges and a choice of tomato sauce or spicy tomato sauce, making it a satisfying main course. The exterior is crisp and golden with a savoury, lightly seasoned breadcrumb crust, while the interior remains juicy and tender. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate source of protein (~17 g/100 g) from the chicken breast, with moderate fat (~10 g/100 g) primarily from the frying process and breadcrumb coating. The carbohydrate content (~15 g/100 g) comes mainly from the breadcrumb coating. Sodium content is notable at approximately 420 mg/100 g, and a full restaurant serving with potato wedges can approach the daily sodium limit for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low-moderate), moderated by the protein and fat content of the chicken. Clinically, portion control is advisable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for those with gluten or egg allergies.

confidence_reason: No exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central for bastoncini di pollo as a named dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for fried breaded chicken breast strips using a composite model. GI is an analogous estimate based on similar breaded meat products from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is well-grounded in approved sources and the food is a straightforward breaded chicken preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent the breaded chicken strip component of the dish; potato wedges and tomato sauce are accompaniments and their nutrients are not included in the per_100g values. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite used for this estimation. 3. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite used for this estimation. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food type. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food type. 6. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0; confirmed zero as chicken breast and breadcrumbs contain negligible vitamin C, confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 7. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; gi_category is low per the ≤55 threshold rule. 8. Halal status is listed as false as it cannot be confirmed without restaurant certification; the restaurant serves Italian cuisine and halal certification is not stated. 9. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Grains and staples' to 'Meat and alternatives' as the primary component is chicken breast fillet. 10. Multilingual names provided for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as this dish is served in Singapore context; these are descriptive translations rather than established local names for this Italian dish.
